title: 38-year-old woman with sudden severe headache
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 doctor in the Emergency Department. Ms Claire Bowman, a 38-year-old teacher, presents with the worst headache of her life that started suddenly 3 hours ago. She is alert and oriented. Please take a history focused on ruling in or out a serious intracranial cause.
examinerNotes: Story is consistent with subarachnoid haemorrhage (thunderclap headache). Candidate must elicit 'worst headache of my life', sudden onset, stiff neck, photophobia. Key investigations: urgent CT head then LP if CT negative. No LP until CT done.

patient.name: Claire Bowman
patient.presentingComplaint: Worst headache I have ever had, came on suddenly
patient.reasonForAttendance: My head is absolutely killing me — it came out of nowhere

hpi.site: Back of head and neck, spreading to whole head
hpi.onset: Sudden — 'like being hit with a bat', occurred during light exercise in garden
hpi.character: Severe, constant, like pressure inside the skull
hpi.timing: 3 hours ago, not worsening but not improving
hpi.relievingFactors: Lying still in the dark, nothing has helped significantly
hpi.severity: 10 out of 10 — I have never had a headache like this in my life
hpi.previousEpisodes: History of mild migraines since age 25, but nothing like this

systemsReview.neurological: No weakness, no visual disturbance, no seizures, no loss of consciousness

examination.general: Alert, oriented, distressed, lying very still, shielding eyes from light
examination.neurological: GCS 15, no focal deficits, Kernig's sign positive, neck stiffness present, photophobia

investigations.name: CT Head (non-contrast)
investigations.result: Hyperdense blood visible in basal cisterns and Sylvian fissures. Star-shaped hyperdensity in suprasellar cistern. No hydrocephalus. No midline shift.
investigations.interpretation: SUBARACHNOID HAEMORRHAGE confirmed. Blood in basal cisterns characteristic of aneurysmal SAH. Urgent neurosurgical referral required.

patient.emotionalState: Frightened and in severe pain. Trying to stay still. Very worried. May mention that this is nothing like her usual migraines.
patient.communicationStyle: Answers in short sentences due to severe pain. Cooperative. Winces at bright light.
hiddenConcerns: Am I going to be okay? Is this a brain bleed?
My mother had a brain aneurysm — is this the same thing?
I have two young children at home
doNotVolunteer: Family history of aneurysm — only if specifically asked about family history of neurological or vascular conditions
That this is different from her usual migraines — only if asked to compare
Severity score — only if asked on a scale of 0 to 10

primaryDiagnosis: Subarachnoid Haemorrhage (SAH) due to ruptured intracranial aneurysm

dataGathering.criterion: Establishes nature of headache — thunderclap onset
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Asks about onset speed, identifies sudden maximum-intensity onset ('like being hit')
Asks about neck stiffness, photophobia, phonophobia, Kernig's sign
Specifically asks if this is different from previous headaches

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about loss of consciousness or focal neurology
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Asks about LOC, seizures, weakness, speech problems, visual disturbance
Orders CT head as priority investigation

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about family history of aneurysm or SAH
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Specifically asks about family history of aneurysm, SAH, or polycystic kidney disease

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: States subarachnoid haemorrhage as primary differential

clinicalManagement.criterion: States LP only after CT — never before
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Correctly sequences CT before LP; does not recommend LP before CT
Refers to neurosurgery/neurology for aneurysm management

clinicalManagement.criterion: Manages pain and provides supportive care

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Acknowledges severity, reassures patient is safe
Explains what tests are needed and why
Responds to fear and family concern

learningObjectives: Recognise thunderclap headache as a red flag requiring urgent investigation
Differentiate SAH from migraine using history alone
Sequence CT head before lumbar puncture correctly
